exemplar.title:
Is there a significant association between the limpets (Patella vulgata) and barnacles 
(Cirripedia) in the lower shore and upper shore of Cobo, Guernsey in June 2024?

detailedExemplarData.title:
Is there a significant association between the limpets (Patella vulgata) and barnacles 
(Cirripedia) in the lower shore and upper shore of Cobo, Guernsey in June 2024?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report uses appropriate ESS terminology consistently
Logical structure with clear headings and table of contents guides the reader
weaknesses: Formatting errors (blank tables, misplaced captions) and OCR artifacts interrupt flow
Bibliography formatting is inconsistent, and occasional verbosity detracts from concision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization, terminology usage, and coherence each meet band 2 descriptors. The combination of sustained organizational strengths and intermittent disruptions supports a holistic score of 2.

strengths: Clear and focused, testable research question framed for chi-square analysis
Effective outline linking rocky shore productivity and abiotic factors to species interactions
weaknesses: Environmental context lacks recent or local quantitative data to deepen discussion
Connections between ecological drivers and research question omit factors such as wave exposure and predation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question is highly focused and coherent (strand at band 5), but the contextual discussion and connection strands fall within band 3â4 due to general descriptions and some omissions. On balance, the evidence aligns with the holistic 3â4 descriptor, supporting a final score of 4.

strengths: Well-designed, repeatable method appropriate for collecting the abundance data needed
Sampling strategy is clearly justified to avoid bias across distinct shore zones
weaknesses: Risk and ethical considerations omit ecological impact assessments and permitting requirements
Lack of detail on controlling tidal height and time of day reduces repeatability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design and sampling strategy both meet band 5 descriptors, but the risk/ethical strand is only outlined (band 3). The overall performance sits in the 3â4 band, warranting a holistic score of 4.

strengths: Method evaluation identifies key strengths (randomisation, sample size) and links these to reliability
Proposed improvements (more sites, seasonal studies) address major spatial and temporal limitations
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusion lacks deeper environmental context linking
Discussion of methodological limitations omits tidal variability and quadrat placement accuracy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands consistently meet the band 3 descriptor (meaningful but not comprehensive evaluation and improvements). The balanced performance across conclusion evaluation, method critique and extensions justifies a score of 3.

strengths: Data analysis (means, ratios, chi-square) is executed correctly with clear interpretation
Conclusions are explicitly linked to statistical results and ecological adaptation
weaknesses: Diagrams and tables have omissions (missing labels, units, blank entries, no error bars)
Minor calculation errors (degrees of freedom, incomplete chi-square components) reduce clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With data presentation at band 3 and analysis/conclusion at bands 4â5, the overall performance aligns with the 3â4 holistic descriptor. Strength in analysis and conclusion outweighs presentation flaws, supporting a score of 4.

strengths: Application proposal (Marine Protected Areas) is clearly linked to findings and well justified
Some relevant strengths and weaknesses of the solution are evaluated
weaknesses: Solution evaluation omits enforcement challenges and ecological trade-offs
Critical analysis of social and economic implications is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal meets band 3 but the solution evaluation aligns with band 2. Overall, the criterion performance fits the holistic 2 descriptor, warranting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Appropriate ESS terminology (e.g., intertidal zone, abiotic factors, chi-square) is used regularly.  Nevertheless, verbosity and typographical clutter (math fonts in tables) reduce concision.  This meets one aspect (terminology) but not both, fitting the 2-mark level.

The evaluation comments on reliability, sample size, and counting errors, and links these limitations to the strength of the conclusion.  Discussion of wider environmental implications is limited.  Thus the strand meets descriptors for âevaluates â¦ with omissionsâ, earning 3.

The student explicitly accepts the alternative hypothesis based on the chi-square value and links patterns to adaptive explanations.  The conclusion is clearly supported by the statistical result and observed trends.  Some uncertainty is acknowledged but not quantified; nonetheless the interpretation is valid and well supported, fitting the lower end of the top band (5).

Means, ratios and a chi-square test are carried out correctly in principle; calculations are shown, and trends are identified.  A minor error in degrees of freedom (2 should be 1) and lack of confidence limits represent partial omissions, but the core analyses are correct, so a high-band 4 is justified.

Improvements (more sites, different seasons) and an extension study are suggested, addressing key weaknesses.  Suggestions are meaningful but not detailed enough to be âsignificant modifications addressing major weaknessesâ; therefore the 3-4 band is appropriate, scored at 3.

The step-by-step procedure (random coordinates within a 20 m Ã 20 m plot, 15 quadrats per zone, counting verified by photos) is repeatable and appropriate for collecting abundance data needed for a chi-square test.  While replication could be larger and tidal height is not precisely controlled, the design will still yield sufficient relevant data.  These minor weaknesses justify 5 rather than the full 6.

Connections between the environmental issue (species interactions under changing abiotic conditions) and the research question are described: productivity supports barnacles, desiccation risk explains limpet distribution, etc.  Links are logical but not comprehensiveâfactors such as wave exposure or predation are only briefly mentioned and not fully integrated.  The strand therefore fits the âdescribes with some omissionsâ descriptor, meriting 4.

The research question is clearly stated, focused on a single measurable relationship (association between two species across two shore zones) and specifies location and time.  It is framed as a testable association suitable for a chi-square test, showing both coherence and focus.  Minor wording redundancies (e.g. repeating speciesâ scientific and common names) do not detract from the clarity, but the phrasing could be tighter, so a secure 5 rather than 6 is appropriate.

Strengths (reduced human interference) and weaknesses (costs, impact on recreation and fisheries) of MPAs are evaluated, but ecological trade-offs or enforcement challenges are not deeply analysed.  Several relevant points are covered, matching âevaluates some relevant strengths and weaknessesâ, for 2 marks.

The report follows a logical IA structure with headings and table of contents, but occasional formatting errors (blank tables, misplaced captions) interrupt flow.  Organisation is present but not sustained seamlessly, corresponding to the 2-mark descriptor.

Strengths (randomisation, identification sheets) and weaknesses (observer bias, hidden specimens, limited spatial scope) are described, but not all limitationsâe.g., tidal timing and quadrat placement accuracyâare covered, nor are impacts quantified.  This aligns with a descriptive rather than comprehensive evaluation, warranting 3.

The student outlines the environmental importance of rocky coastlines, productivity, upwelling, climate change and pollution, linking these ideas to the organisms studied.  The discussion provides context but remains fairly general and descriptive; quantitative local information or recent data are absent.  The treatment goes beyond a brief outline yet stops short of a full, critical discussion, fitting solidly in the 3-4 band at the upper end.

A random sampling strategy is explained and justified as a way to avoid bias; coordinates generation and quadrat placement are described.  The choice of two discrete shore levels is rationalised in terms of differing abiotic conditions.  Justification could be strengthened with tidal-height definitions or stratification, but the rationale provided meets the âjustifiesâ requirement, earning 5.

Physical risks (slippery rocks, sharp surfaces) are listed with sensible mitigations.  Ethical disturbance is acknowledged but only briefly; no consideration of broader ecological impact or permitting is offered.  The coverage moves beyond a list yet lacks the detail expected for comprehensive description, so the mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Raw-data tables and comparative bar charts are presented for both shores.  However, one processed-data table (mean limpets) is blank, axis labels on graphs are minimal, and units/uncertainty are absent, indicating omissions.  All relevant data are not fully or flawlessly displayed, matching the âappropriate diagrams with some omissionsâ level.

A clear proposalâestablishing Marine Protected Areasâis linked to reducing anthropogenic impact on rocky-shore interactions.  The student explains how IA data could help site selection, providing a logical justification based on findings.  This meets the requirement for a justified application, earning full marks (3).

Argumentation generally follows a logical progression from method to data to conclusion.  However, gaps such as the missing processed-data table and incorrect degrees of freedom momentarily hinder reader comprehension.  The essay is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, meriting 2.

annotations.comment: Using photos and hand tally counters to verify counts demonstrates methodological rigor and helps minimize observer errors.
Evaluation section effectively identifies strengths in randomisation and sample size, reinforcing methodological reliability.
The research question is clearly stated, focused, and testable with a chi-square analysis, demonstrating coherence and specificity.
The table of contents lacks page numbers, which hinders navigation. Include corresponding page references for each section to improve reader orientation.
Introduction effectively defines limpets and barnacles, but would benefit from recent or local quantitative data on their abundance in Guernsey to strengthen context.
Figure 1 caption is minimal; include scale bars and source details (e.g. photographer, date) to enhance reproducibility and proper attribution.
The environmental context describes productivity factors well but remains general; integrate specific metrics (e.g. upwelling nutrient concentrations, pollution levels) for depth.
Local and global context outlines relevance but would be stronger by referencing specific conservation measures or biodiversity statistics for Guernseyâs coasts.
The alternative hypothesis is concisely phrased and aligns with your chosen chi-square test, demonstrating a strong link to your research question.
Listing quadrat placement via random number generation as an independent variable conflates method with variable; focus on shore zone as sole independent variable.
Controlled variables are listed generally; specify how factors like tidal height or time of day were kept constant to improve repeatability.
Equipment list includes â1Ã Random number generatorâ without detail; clarify device or app used, including accuracy and seed method.
The paragraph on sampling method ends abruptly, suggesting truncated text. Ensure complete sentences and clarify the 20Ã20 m rationale.
Figure 2âs identification sheets are useful, but adding a scale bar and image source (date, photographer) strengthens documentation.
Method steps omit control of tidal state; specify how tide height and time were standardized to reduce abiotic variability.
Risk assessment covers physical hazards well but omits ecological disturbance beyond lifting rocks; include potential impacts on non-target intertidal fauna and flora.
Ethical considerations acknowledge disruption but lack mention of permits or guidelines for intertidal research; address permitting and minimal-impact protocols.
Raw data table contains numerous formatting artifacts (overlines, Z, blank entries) that obscure values; reformat to present clear counts and correct OCR errors.
Qualitative observations effectively link distribution patterns to species adaptations, enriching the quantitative findings.
Graphs on page 11 lack axis labels, units, and error bars, reducing interpretability; include these elements to present data fully.
Processed data table is blank and labelled oddly, indicating incomplete work; ensure all relevant summary statistics are presented without formatting placeholders.
Mean calculation is clearly shown and rounded to two decimal places, demonstrating transparent data processing.
Chi-squared formula is explicitly included, improving clarity of analysis procedure and aiding reproducibility.
Contingency table correctly displays totals for expected value calculation, strengthening the basis for your chi-squared test.
Chi-square calculation table is missing the final upper-shore barnacle term and its contribution; complete all four components for transparency.
Degrees of freedom calculation is incorrect; $$df=(2-1)(2-1)=1$$, not 2. Correct this to validate your significance test.
Chi-squared distribution table includes unused rows and columns; streamline to df=1 and alpha=0.05 to focus critical value selection.
Remove the stray â13,â text, which appears to be an OCR artifact, to maintain coherence and professionalism.
Conclusion succinctly links chi-square result to hypothesis decision and acknowledges uncertainty, demonstrating strong analytical reasoning.
Consider discussing how your sample size influences statistical power and confidence intervals to deepen your evaluation of uncertainty.
Weakness discussion should include tidal variability and seasonal effects as method limitations to provide a more comprehensive critique.
Proposed improvements to sample multiple sites and seasons are meaningful and directly address major spatial and temporal limitations.
Marine Protected Area proposal is well justified by findings, demonstrating thoughtful application of results to real-world conservation.
Solution evaluation omits discussion of enforcement challenges and ecological trade-offs in MPAs; include these to strengthen critical analysis.
Bibliography entries show inconsistent formatting and missing access dates; apply a uniform citation style (e.g. APA or MLA) throughout.
